structData.title: Bid floor configuration — why bids lose all auctions
structData.content:
A bid floor is the minimum CPM price a publisher or SSP will accept for an impression. If currentBid is below bidFloor, your ad will never win an auction even if there is zero competition. The SSP filters the impression before it enters the auction.

HOW TO DIAGNOSE
Call getCampaignStatus and compare two fields:
  currentBid  — what your DSP is submitting
  bidFloor    — the minimum the SSP will accept
If currentBid < bidFloor, you are losing 100% of auctions.

COMMON CAUSES OF BID BELOW FLOOR
1. Publisher raised their floor price after you set the campaign bid. Floor prices change regularly, especially for premium inventory.
2. Your line item bid was set when the campaign launched and has not been reviewed since.
3. You are targeting premium inventory categories (connected TV, video, high-viewability display) which have higher floors than standard display.
4. Your DSP applies a platform fee or data fee on top of your bid before submitting to the SSP. This reduces your effective bid. Example: you set $1.50 CPM, DSP adds $0.30 platform fee, effective bid = $1.20. If floor is $1.40 you will lose.
5. The deal has a fixedPrice floor set at contract time that is higher than your current bid.

HOW TO FIX
1. Get the bidFloor value from getCampaignStatus.
2. Calculate effective bid: yourBid minus platformFee minus dataFee.
3. Set new bid to at least bidFloor + $0.10 buffer to account for floor fluctuations.
4. For PMP deals: check getDealStatus fixedPrice and ensure your bid meets or exceeds it.
5. For open auction: consider enabling bid shading if your DSP supports it — this optimises bids while staying above floors.

FLOOR PRICE TYPES
  Hard floor: SSP rejects any bid below this price. Common for direct deals.
  Soft floor: SSP accepts lower bids but prioritises bids above the floor. Common in open auction.
  Dynamic floor: Floor changes in real time based on historical clearing prices. Your DSP should support bid landscape data to stay competitive.
